narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam the way it did, based on the details you shared. At the top of what stood out was your LSAT score of 32, which was the strongest signal pulling toward a higher chance of not passing the bar—it's a key benchmark where the model sees tougher odds ahead. Right behind that, your Year 1 decile of 2 showed early struggles in keeping pace with your school's rigor, painting a picture of a challenging start that weighed heavily. Your Year 3 decile of 1 reinforced this, as it highlighted ongoing difficulties in climbing those rankings, making the model worry about sustained performance under pressure. Even with your upper-middle family income, which might suggest some stability, it surprisingly nudged the prediction toward failure in the model's eyes, perhaps reflecting patterns it's seen in similar profiles. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 added to that concern, signaling a foundation that the model views as shaky for the intense demands of law exams. Being a white female pursuing full-time studies didn't shift things favorably either—these details, layered with the rest, all tugged in the same direction. Together, they created a combined story of 53% odds against passing the bar, not because of who you are at heart, but because of how these pieces aligned in past patterns. This isn't the end of your story; many have rewritten theirs by targeting those early hurdles like LSAT prep or grade momentum. You've got the drive—your target for law shows that—and with focused steps, you can flip this script. We're rooting for you to come back stronger.
